title: Auburn police seek missing Mass. woman last seen in Florida
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Jennifer Remillard, 47, has been missing since January 31, last seen in Sarasota, Florida.
Remillard is 5'9", about 130 pounds, with blonde hair, and may be driving a gray 2012 Toyota Camry.
Auburn Police ask anyone with information to contact them at 508-832-7777 or via anonymous email.

title: Boston’s innovation economy hits a new milestone in global rankings
author: Beth Treffeisen
bullets: Five Boston-area companies named on LexisNexis's 'Top 100 Global Innovators' list, a record for the region.
Orna Therapeutics, a first-time honoree, was acquired by Eli Lilly for $2.4 billion.
Boston now ties with L.A. for third in the U.S. for most companies on the list.

title: Is Scituate native Conor Garland a worthwhile trade target for Bruins?
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Conor Garland, originally from Scituate, is linked to the Bruins in trade talks ahead of Friday's NHL deadline.
Garland, 30, has a $6 million cap hit through 2031-32 and averages over 40 points per season.
The Bruins are assessing if Garland fits their team timeline and trade goals amidst ongoing playoff ambitions.

title: Recent homes sales in Greater Boston (Mar. 4)
author: The Boston Globe
bullets: Abington sold a 1,040 sq ft Colonial at 1210 Washington St. for $500,000; built in 1950, with 2 bedrooms.
Acton featured sales include a 2,726 sq ft Colonial at 7 Concetta Circle for $1,175,000 on a 40,001 sq ft lot.
Boston condo at 100 Belvidere St. #8H sold for $3,700,000; high-rise, 1,692 sq ft, with 2 bedrooms and 3 baths.

title: Patriots might have shot at top free-agent receiver after Colts’ franchise-tag gamble
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Indianapolis Colts chose to apply a transition tag on QB Daniel Jones, making Alec Pierce available in free agency.
Pierce led the NFL in yards per catch in 2024 and 2025, potentially appealing to the Patriots' strong offense.
New England could afford Pierce but may need to restructure Stefon Diggs' contract to clear cap space.

title: Vermont postal worker allegedly threw away mail she was supposed to deliver for months
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Natasha Morisseau, 34, was accused of discarding mail meant for delivery while working as a USPS mail carrier in North Troy.
Police found opened and unopened mail, including packages and holiday cards, in a dumpster and Morisseau's mail vehicle.
Morisseau faces nine petty larceny counts and five unlawful mischief counts; she is due in Vermont Superior Court on March 17.

title: Man in his 80s, dog dead in Foxborough fire
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: An elderly man and his dog died in a house fire on Granite Street in Foxborough early Tuesday morning.
Foxborough, Mansfield, and Plainville firefighters responded quickly and contained the fire within an hour despite major damage.
The fire's cause is under investigation by local and state officials, including the State Fire Marshal.

title: Patriots players brought strippers, hookahs to celebrate at a Boston restaurant, its owner says. He’s now facing a license violation.
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Liquor license of Estella's restaurant in Boston is at risk after hosting Patriots players with strippers and hookahs.
Owner Helder George Brandao says Patriots players brought their own alcohol and hookahs, causing the after-hours disruption.
Boston Licensing Board to vote on potential suspension or revocation of Estella's liquor license due to the incident.

title: Mass. students place No. 1 nationwide for AP scores, break all-time record
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Massachusetts students achieved the highest nationwide scores on AP exams, with 35.8% scoring 3 or higher.
The state has the highest percentage of Black or African American students taking AP exams, behind only Washington D.C.
Massachusetts has subsidized over 90,783 AP exams for nearly 50,000 low-income students to increase college credit opportunities.

title: ‘Forced to take this pay raise’: Quincy city councilors are collecting the raise they promised to repeal
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Quincy City Council raises, from $29,128.65 to $44,500, were enacted by the previous council without public input.
New councilors, elected on promises to repeal the raises, plan to donate their additional income to charities and nonprofits.
Proposed raise for Quincy Mayor Thomas Koch was adjusted to $225,000 and delayed until 2028, following public opposition.
